content.text: As organisations scale their investment in AI agents across multiple product teams, the knowledge needed to build reliable, safe, and effective systems begins to fragment — each team rediscovers the same failure modes, reinvents the same evaluation patterns, and duplicates tooling that a shared forum could distribute in a single hour. Cross-team improvement forums and guilds are the structural answer to this fragmentation.

A cross-team improvement forum is a regularly scheduled meeting — typically bi-weekly or monthly — attended by representatives from every team building or operating AI agents in the organisation. Its purpose is to surface learnings from individual team retrospectives, disseminate best practices, coordinate on shared infrastructure, and maintain a shared view of the organisation's agent health across all products.

A guild is the persistent community that owns and runs the forum. The AI guild (sometimes called a chapter, community of practice, or center of excellence) is a cross-functional group of practitioners — prompt engineers, ML engineers, SREs, and product managers with AI experience — who meet periodically, maintain shared standards, and serve as the organisational muscle memory for agentic AI delivery.

The two concepts are related but distinct. The forum is the event; the guild is the social structure that makes the forum sustainable. A forum without a guild tends to collapse after a few months because no one owns its agenda or follows through on cross-team decisions. A guild without a forum has no mechanism to share knowledge and will fragment into informal conversations.

Individual team retrospectives are excellent at surfacing team-level issues. They are poor at distributing that knowledge to other teams. The consequence is redundant failure: Team A discovers that streaming tool-call results reduces perceived latency and writes it up in their retro notes; six weeks later, Team B independently discovers the same thing and wastes two days of engineering time getting to the same conclusion.

This redundancy is not merely inefficient; for safety-relevant findings it is dangerous. If Team A encounters a prompt injection pattern and documents it only in their own retro, Team B remains exposed. Cross-team forums are the mechanism that converts individual team learning into organisational institutional memory.

A second function is coordination. Multiple teams building agents often share infrastructure: a retrieval system, an evaluation harness, a model gateway, or a shared prompt library. Without a cross-team forum, changes to that shared infrastructure happen without coordination, causing downstream breakage. The forum provides a standing venue for infrastructure changes to be announced and discussed before they are made.
A best practice discovered by one team reaches all teams in the next forum cycle rather than propagating via informal conversation over weeks or months.

Guilds can maintain lightweight standards (evaluation templates, prompt style guides, safety checklists) that teams adopt voluntarily because they trust the guild, not because a central function mandated them.

When multiple teams surface the same tooling gap (e.g. no shared prompt versioning system), the forum creates a business case for a shared investment rather than each team building a bespoke solution.
Near-misses and safety-adjacent events that would be invisible in a single team's retro become visible as patterns when aggregated across teams at the forum.

Guild membership gives practitioners visibility across the organisation, opportunities to present and receive feedback on their work, and access to a broader peer network than a single product team provides.

The fundamental tension in any guild structure is between the value of sharing and the cost of coordination. A guild meeting that is too frequent becomes an additional overhead meeting that teams resent; one that is too infrequent loses momentum and relevance. Most organisations settle on bi-weekly as the minimum effective cadence, but the right frequency depends on the pace of change in the agent ecosystem.

Guild decisions are not enforceable across teams. A team that disagrees with a guild recommendation can simply ignore it. This makes the guild dependent on influence and trust rather than authority, which requires more sophisticated leadership.
Guilds often depend heavily on one or two energetic organisers. If those individuals leave, the guild collapses unless organisational leadership formally maintains it.

As guild reputation grows, it attracts requests beyond its mandate — architectural reviews, individual team roadmap decisions, hiring panels. Without a defined scope, the guild's bandwidth is consumed by activities that dilute its core function.

If only senior engineers attend the forum, the perspectives of junior practitioners, QA engineers, and product managers are missing. Those perspectives often surface the most important usability and reliability issues.

A well-functioning AI guild operates at two levels simultaneously: the forum (scheduled meeting) and the persistent community (asynchronous channels, working groups, shared documents). The forum is where decisions are made visible and learning is shared synchronously; the community is where the work between forums happens.
A standard bi-weekly forum runs 60–75 minutes and follows a consistent agenda. Consistency is essential: participants should be able to prepare without re-reading the agenda each time.
Each team sends one representative who shares a two-minute update: key metric changes, notable incidents, decisions made. Updates are structured, not open conversation.
One team presents a specific learning from the last sprint in depth — a failure mode discovered, a technique validated, a tooling experiment completed. This is the core knowledge-transfer mechanism.
Issues that require cross-team input are raised and discussed. This slot is for questions like "Should we standardise on a single prompt versioning system?" not for status updates.
Updates on active guild projects: a shared evaluation template being drafted, a prompt style guide under review, an infrastructure proposal in progress.
Review last forum's actions, confirm completion or deferral, assign new actions from today's discussion.

Between forums, the guild maintains a Slack or Teams channel where practitioners share links, ask questions, and flag emerging issues. The guild also runs asynchronous working groups: small groups of three to five people who meet separately to produce a specific artefact (a document, a code library, a checklist) and then bring the result to the forum for broader feedback.

attrs.alt: Hub-and-spoke diagram: Central AI Platform Guild connected to five product and platform teams. Solid arrows show teams contributing learnings to the guild; dashed arrows show the guild broadcasting standards back to teams.

content.text: The guild sits at the centre of the knowledge network. Individual teams contribute learnings upward (solid arrows) and receive standards, tools, and decisions back from the guild (dashed arrows). This bidirectional flow is what separates a genuine guild from a broadcast channel: the guild generates value from aggregation, not just from central broadcasting.

Cross-team forums and guilds are worth establishing once an organisation has at least three teams building or operating AI agents. Below that threshold, informal communication is usually sufficient and a formal guild adds overhead without sufficient benefit.
when you observe teams making the same mistakes independently, duplicating tooling, or missing each other's safety findings.
when the forum exists but lacks ownership — when meeting notes go unwritten, action items are not tracked, and each meeting starts from scratch without continuity.
as a top-down mandate without identified practitioners who want to participate. A guild that is formed by decree and populated with reluctant attendees will fail.
Wind down or suspend the forum

if attendance drops consistently below 50% of teams for more than two consecutive cycles. A consistently empty forum signals a structural problem — wrong cadence, wrong agenda, or a mandate mismatch — not a temporary scheduling conflict.
Recruit two to three practitioners from each team who are enthusiastic about knowledge sharing. They do not need to be the most senior people — they need to be motivated.

Write a one-page document specifying what the guild does and does not do, who can join, how decisions are made, and what the forum agenda structure is. Share it with all teams before the first meeting.
Scheduling one meeting and seeing if it recurs naturally does not work. Book the first six recurring sessions at launch. This creates a calendar anchor that teams plan around.
Someone must own the agenda, the notes, and the action registry. This role should rotate every quarter but must be explicit — a guild with a shared ownership illusion effectively has no owner.
Deliver a tangible artefact by day 45.

The guild must demonstrate value early. Identify one thing teams consistently lack — an evaluation template, a prompt review checklist, a shared incident severity guide — and produce it within the first 45 days. This builds credibility.

A two-question survey — "Is this forum worth the time?" and "What would make it more valuable?" — at the 90-day mark prevents the guild from drifting into irrelevance while there is still momentum to correct course.

# AI Agent Guild — Scope and Charter

## Purpose
To accelerate the quality and reliability of AI agent delivery across the
organisation by sharing knowledge, maintaining shared standards, and coordinating
on shared infrastructure.

## In scope
- Retrospective learnings shared across teams
- Shared evaluation templates and prompt style guides
- Coordination on the shared model gateway and retrieval infrastructure
- Safety incident pattern aggregation and response coordination
- Tooling experiments and recommendations

## Out of scope
- Individual team roadmap or staffing decisions
- Architectural approval for team-specific agent designs
- Performance reviews or hiring panels

## Membership
- Core members: one representative per team building AI agents (required to attend forums)
- Extended members: practitioners who attend ad-hoc; invited to async channels
- Guild lead: rotates quarterly; owns agenda, notes, and action registry

## Decision model
- Consensus preferred; guild lead breaks ties
- Decisions are recommendations, not mandates
- Teams may opt out of a recommendation by documenting their reasoning

## Forum cadence
- Bi-weekly, 60 minutes, fixed agenda (see separate doc)
- Notes published within 24 hours of the meeting
- Action items tracked in the Guild Action Registry

# Learning Spotlight: [Team Name] — [Date]

## What we tried
Brief description of the change, experiment, or event.

## Context
- Sprint goal at the time
- System: [agent name / domain]
- Scale: [query volume / number of users affected]

## What happened
Neutral description of the outcome, with data where available.

## Root cause or mechanism
Why did this happen? What is the underlying principle?

## What other teams should know
The actionable takeaway, written for a team that has none of the context.

## Artefacts
Links to: eval data, prompt diff, incident report, code change, runbook update.

## Questions for the forum
Specific questions this team would like input on from the group.

An organisation with five product teams building agents across different domains — customer support, document processing, internal search, data analysis, and scheduling — established an AI guild after observing that each team had independently built its own evaluation harness with different formats, metrics, and tooling. The cost was not just redundant engineering: because the evaluation formats were incompatible, leadership could not get a unified view of agent quality across the product portfolio.

The guild's first project was to produce a shared evaluation schema — a standard JSON structure for test cases and results that all five teams could adopt. The guild ran three working-group sessions over six weeks, produced the schema, and presented it at a forum. Four of the five teams adopted it immediately; the fifth requested one extension to handle a domain-specific output type, which the working group incorporated in a point release.

Within three months, the engineering manager for the portfolio could pull a single dashboard that showed eval pass rates across all five agents, enabling prioritisation conversations that had previously required manual aggregation of five separate reports. The guild had created a multiplier: one artefact produced by six people in six weeks saved hundreds of hours of reporting work and enabled a level of portfolio visibility that had not existed before.
The Guild Becomes a Status Meeting

The most common failure mode is the guild forum devolving into a status update meeting where each team representative gives a project update with no meaningful cross-team discussion. This happens when the agenda does not explicitly protect time for the learning spotlight and open-issues slots, and when the guild lead does not redirect status updates to async channels. If more than 20 minutes of a 60-minute forum is spent on status, restructure the agenda.

Guilds that attempt to enforce standards by organisational authority rather than by demonstrating value lose the cooperation of teams. A team that adopts a guild standard because it improves their velocity will advocate for the guild. A team that adopts a standard under pressure will comply minimally and resist extensions. The guild's influence derives entirely from the quality of the artefacts it produces and the trust it earns, not from hierarchy.
No Async Channel for Between-Forum Questions

If the only guild interaction is the bi-weekly forum, practitioners will not ask questions in the two weeks between sessions — they will either solve problems independently or raise them at the next meeting two weeks later. An always-on async channel where guild members can ask questions and share links is not optional; it is the primary day-to-day value delivery mechanism.
Assign the spotlight slot to teams in a fixed rotation so every team presents every few sessions. This ensures every team invests in documenting their learnings at a regular cadence.

Treat guild projects (artefacts to produce, standards to draft) as a prioritised list. When the guild finishes one project, pick the next from the backlog rather than starting a new ad-hoc initiative. This prevents the guild from taking on more than it can complete.
Forum notes shared the day after the meeting have much higher readership than notes shared three days later. Assign note-taking explicitly, not to "whoever has time."

Track three simple metrics: forum attendance rate by team, number of artefacts produced per quarter, and a biannual member satisfaction score. These make the guild's health visible without excessive overhead.
Invite safety engineers as regular observers.
Having a safety or security engineer attend the forum regularly, even as a non-speaking observer, creates a channel for safety concerns to be heard at the point where cross-team decisions are made.

A guild that covers three to five teams can run a single unified forum. As the number of teams grows beyond eight or ten, a single forum becomes too large for effective discussion and too broad for relevant learning spotlights (a spotlight on retrieval optimisation is irrelevant to a team building a scheduling agent).

The solution is a tiered guild structure. Domain-specific sub-guilds (e.g. a retrieval guild, a safety guild, an orchestration guild) run their own forums focused on their domain. A meta-guild or steering group composed of one representative from each sub-guild meets monthly to coordinate cross-domain decisions and share artefacts between sub-guilds.
are more relevant to individual practitioners and maintain higher attendance rates than a catch-all forum.
prevents the sub-guilds from duplicating each other's work and ensures organisation-wide concerns (a new model provider, a major security policy change) are propagated to all sub-guilds.
The async channel layer. A dedicated channel for the guild (e.g. #ai-guild) plus domain-specific channels (e.g. #ai-guild-safety) enables both broad and focused asynchronous discussion.
Documentation layers where guild artefacts (standards, templates, checklists, forum notes) are published and maintained. A well-organised guild wiki is a primary deliverable.

For shared code artefacts — evaluation harness code, shared prompt libraries, schema definitions — a guild-owned GitHub organisation or repository allows version control, PR reviews across teams, and change visibility.
For guilds that want to run public or cross-company events (e.g. an AI agent practitioners meetup), event management tools allow broader outreach beyond the immediate organisation.

Cross-team forums present a specific information-security challenge: participants from different teams may have different security clearance levels or work in regulated domains where sharing certain information across teams is restricted. A support agent team may be prohibited from sharing user query samples even anonymised; a financial agent team may have PCI compliance constraints that govern what can be discussed in a forum attended by non-PCI-certified participants.

The guild scope document should specify what categories of information can be shared at the forum (learnings, patterns, metrics) and what cannot (production query samples, user identifiers, IP-sensitive system architectures).
Learning spotlights should reference agent behaviour patterns and metric trends, not specific user interactions. Any example query used in a spotlight should be synthetic or structurally anonymised.

Notes may contain discussion of system weaknesses or safety near-misses. Publish them to the guild membership only, not to the broader organisation, unless the guild has reviewed the notes for sensitive content.

Think of the guild as a collective immune system. Each team building an AI agent encounters threats — prompt injection attempts, evaluation failures, model regressions, tool outages. When a team encounters a threat, it develops a response (a new test, a guardrail, a monitoring alert). Without a guild, that immunity stays inside the one team. With a guild, the response is shared, and every team becomes resistant to the same threat.

The forum is the mechanism of transmission. The guild is the tissue that ensures transmission actually happens regularly and systematically rather than opportunistically. Just as a biological immune system weakens without regular exposure and stimulation, an organisation's collective knowledge base weakens if no mechanism compels its regular update and distribution.
A cross-team forum is the event; a guild is the persistent social structure that makes the forum sustainable. Both are needed for durable knowledge sharing.
Forums convert individual team learnings into organisational institutional memory; without them, each team rediscovers the same failure modes independently.
Establish a guild once three or more teams are building agents; below that threshold, informal communication is usually sufficient.
The guild operates through influence, not authority. Its power comes from the quality of the artefacts it produces and the trust it earns from member teams, not from organisational mandate.
The guild lead role must be explicit and assigned, not shared implicitly. Rotate it quarterly to prevent single points of failure.
Protect the learning spotlight slot from status-update drift; it is the primary knowledge-transfer mechanism and the core reason the forum exists.

Establish information-sharing rules before the first forum: participants from different teams may have different data-access rights, and sensitive system details must be anonymised before being shared.
